data.title: GM Clings To Its First-In-Class Status
data.description:
By Breakingviews

General Motors (GM) is riding easy - for now. After years of struggling to transform into an electrified rival to Tesla (TSLA), the Detroit-based automaker is defying expectations, pumping up prices and edging ahead of its old-guard peers. Problem is, while the company led by Mary Barra is well-suited to this moment, the moment might be ending.

Third-quarter results unveiled Tuesday show revenue, at nearly $49 billion, beating analysts’ expectations by 8%, while operating profit came in 22% higher, according to Visible Alpha. GM’s shares leaped over 9% and are trouncing competitors’ year-to-date.

Barra’s lieutenants have, in the past, signaled that new vehicle prices might not hold. It seems reasonable: GM juiced billions in profit from pricing since the pandemic crushed supply back in 2020. Interest rate hikes make car loans more expensive. Industry-wide, prices have declined for 11 months, Cox Automotive reckons.

GM’s results, however, defy the trend. While arch-rival Ford Motor (F) remains 17% below its 2018 sales volume, GM clawed back to a 5% deficit. Its electric effort - stumbling for years as battery plants scaled up - is gaining traction. Barra expects to produce 200,000 EVs this year, which should finally cover their per-unit costs. The company’s share of the U.S. EV market rose to 9.3%, according to Cox, nearly double its mid-2023 slice.

Even autonomous taxi unit Cruise has promise. In the penalty box since a pedestrian collision last year, it nearly halved its losses to $400 million this quarter. But it’s rolled out new testing markets; Barra says unsupervised rides will resume by year-end. As Alphabet’s (GOOGL, GOOG) Waymo proves robotaxis’ viability, topping 100,000 weekly rides, that’s a tantalizing prospect. GM plans to work with Uber Technologies (UBER) and is looking for a new source of funding. A solid strategic partner could help snag more customers.

Yet, uncertainty keeps rising. Republican Donald Trump could win the White House, and is skeptical of EV subsidies that are providing an $800 million benefit to GM this year. Tesla boss Elon Musk is pushing low-cost robotaxi technology that, while a long shot, could upend the industry.

Even GM remains cautious: despite predicting an up to $4 billion profitability boost from improving EV economics, it expects 2025’s results to match this year’s, implying declines elsewhere. At a mere 5 times expected earnings, its valuation pales to Tesla’s 89 times and indicates market skepticism of any upside. Barra might seem first-in-class among the aged automakers looking to catch Musk. Holding on to that lead could get a lot tougher.

Context News

General Motors said on October 22 that it generated $48.8 billion in revenue in the third quarter, up 10.5% year-over-year and some 8% above analysts’ expectations, according to Visible Alpha. The Detroit-based automaker reported $4.1 billion in operating income, adjusted to exclude results from its finance arm and various one-off expenses, 22% above estimates. Benefits from pricing boosted profitability by $1 billion from the prior year. The company raised the bottom end of expected operating profit for the full year to $14 billion, from $13 billion previously.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Kemper: Overly Conservative Management Limits Upside Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Kemper Corporation (NYSE:KMPR) is a solid insurance company with reasonable underwriting profitability. The numbers are mostly unexciting, with some decent growth in book value and a modest improvement in the underlying combined ratio to 89.6% in the latest quarter. I think this stock is already fairly priced and has no more upside at these levels. Also, I am critical of management of being overly conservative and letting peers largely surpass Kemper uncontested. Overall, I think with low to mid single digit expected growth in earnings, the current forward PE of 12.62x seems quite reasonable and warrants a hold rating.

Company Overview

As a diversified insurance business, Kemper provides "automobile, life, and other insurance products to individuals and businesses" according to the 10-K. They collect premiums, invest it in relatively safe fixed income securities, and focus on maintaining a profitable combined loss ratio. It's a pretty run-of-the-mill insurance company that focuses only on business in the United States.

They conduct their business in two segments: Specialty Property & Casualty, and Life Insurance. However, in the third quarter of 2023 Kemper announced the closing of their preferred property and casualty business, "As a result, the Company will no longer provide preferred automobile, homeowners insurance or other personal insurance".

The decision to exit this part of the market was a benefit to the bottom line. Over the course of time, the combined loss ratio went from 100.5% in Q3 of 2023 to 89.6% in Q2, 2024, demonstrating that management was indeed right to release capital from this loss-making business.

For life insurance, Kemper offers life insurance through agents, but makes up a small percent of total premiums collected for the business. According to the annual report,

Earned premiums from the life insurance segment accounted for 7%, of the Company’s consolidated insurance premiums earned in 2023 and 2022, respectively, and 6% of the Company’s insurance premium earned in 2021.

Overall, investors can see this well diversified insurance business is mostly leaning into their specialty P&C business for its earnings. They invest the bulk of their premiums in safe fixed income assets that are short-term corporate bonds. The investment portfolio is a little more risky than I'd like, with only 71% of their fixed maturities rated A or higher according to their investor presentation.

Nonetheless, for the moment, the business is performing smoothly with pretty run-of-the-mill fundamentals. I think a hold rating is appropriate as there is nothing extraordinary happening at the moment, and modest earnings growth is already fully priced in.

Management Should Be More Aggressive

Overall, this insurance company is well diversified but lacks any exceptional growth or underwriting profitability. Book value inched up from $39.22 to $41.46 over the past year, and the combined loss ratio has improved 12.4% over the past few quarters. While this is good news, the market is already pricing these improvements in, and I'm unconvinced that there's much more improvement down the line.

Kemper seems to be quite conservative in their guidance, showing minimal optimism from management's side. In their earnings call transcript they mention,

Specialty P&C's improved combined ratio allowed for further expansion of our production initiatives, resulting in a 4.6% sequential increase in PIF, exceeding expectations we discussed last quarter. We anticipate low single-digit PIF growth for the remainder of the year.

This low single digit sequential growth in PIF signals to me that management is very conservative and running a steady ship. They are good at taking prudent risk, but this also means that the fundamentals aren't extraordinary compared to peers. I think they aggressively need to ramp up the PIF dramatically to take advantage of hard markets right now, and management is hinting on executing this plan,

As we discussed last quarter, now that we've restored profitability, we pivoted our focus to PIF growth. Given the volatile market environment, we continue to incorporate an additional margin of safety in our business practices.

Judging from the rhetoric, guidance, and recent numbers, I get the sense that management is overly conservative in running their business. Based on YoY declines in PIF, lackluster book value growth, and a decrease of 7.4% in Specialty P&C Earned Premium YoY, these numbers are not exciting to me considering this favorable insurance market. I believe that this business could earn much more if it were more aggressive in pursuing policies, growing their earned premiums, and thus increasing their ROE. There's a time to be defensive, and right now, I think Kemper needs to turn more aggressive and catch profitable growth when the tide is in their favor.

Well Capitalized, Strong Balance Sheet

Kemper seems well reserved and capitalized to afford to take on more risk. According to the investor presentation, the balance sheet is pretty strong.

With $1.1 billion in liquidity and well capitalized insurance companies, it is a mystery to me why management isn't more aggressive. They are (metaphorically) sitting at the poker table, flush with cash and playing their cards way too conservatively, in my opinion. A debt to capital ratio of even 30% doesn't seem too concerning, so I think management should pursue growth aggressively to improve earnings.

Management's target combined loss ratios are rather conservative, and in my opinion limit the true ROE potential of this business. They mention in their transcript,

Third, last quarter, we told you that our long-term goal for specialty auto was to produce a 96% combined ratio or better and grow as much as possible within that. This is a long-term operating parameter for this business.

To me, 96% in this insurance market is not a goal but a hindrance for the business. I'm not sure why management would purposely set a long-term operating parameter that actually limits the profitability of their business, considering with their current balance sheet and capital profile they can afford to take on more policies and risk. Overall, my opinion is that Kemper could do a lot better than it is now, and management is overly conservative in running their insurance business.

Kemper Lags Its Peers

While other insurance companies take advantage of the market opportunities and grow, Kemper is currently lagging its peers in terms of revenue growth and return on equity.

I'm not an activist investor, but these charts to me says it all. Kemper has barely moved over the past decade, while every one of their peers continues to trounce Kemper in terms of revenue growth and return on equity. I'm convinced Kemper fell asleep at the wheel, and their management team could be more aggressive. Overall, Kemper is far too conservatively run and has let their peers fly right past them.

Valuation - $50 Fair Value

I'm going to project lackluster growth and mediocre profitability, given management's conservative philosophy in running the business. I expect ROEs to stay at or above 10%, based on management's own rough guidance,

Second, I'm going to add some clarity and insight to our 2024 guidance. In late 2023, we said we would achieve a 10% or greater ROE in 2024. Given our strong first half results, let me be clear that we expect to solidly beat that 10% for the year.

With shareholder's equity of roughly $2.7 billion, I think earnings will come in at $270 million annually. Divide by shares outstanding of 64 million gets me $4 EPS, rounded down. Apply a 13x forward PE multiple (which is the sector median) gets me $50 fair value, rounded down.

Investors can see the stock may be slightly overvalued, but it's nothing to be super alarmed about. However, I think management is not aggressive enough to truly unlock the full potential of Kemper. If they were more aggressive in chasing growth, the business could be potentially worth more than $50 per share. The stock is a hold for now because the earnings seem already fairly priced in, and I see no indication that they will grow significantly. With a 12-13x FWD earnings multiple currently, I think the market is fairly valuing this company.

Risks

Like any insurance company, Kemper faces risks from catastrophe losses arising from natural disasters. Recently, hurricanes in Florida have the whole industry braced for impact, with some sources suggesting Hurricane Milton could cost the insurance industry up to $50 billion. As a property insurer, Kemper may be somewhat impacted by the recent hurricane and take a modest hit.

With recent hurricanes, reinsurance may be getting more expensive in the future. Reinsurers know how much risk they are taking, so they could be charging more aggressive rates to insurers, costing Kemper more money to buy reinsurance.

The insurance business is highly competitive. I feel that management needs to be more aggressive, and that other companies with more aggressive management teams can trounce Kemper in terms of growth and profitability. Other insurance carriers may be more attractive investments and leave Kemper unrecognized by the investor community.

Hold Kemper

I feel that management is limiting the true upside potential of the business with their overly conservative approach. They should aggressively pursue customers, and even perhaps drop the life insurance business completely. Kemper is rather mediocre considering many of its peers have better track records of profitability and growth in the same industry, leading me to believe management is far too conservative in running their ship.

data.title: The Next Big Theme: October 2024
data.description:
Defense Technology

Drone Systems Gain Further Momentum, Signaling Shift in Modern Warfare Tactics

U.S. drone supplier AeroVironment inked a $1 billion contract with the U.S. Army to supply its Switchblade loitering munitions for infantry battalions.1 Switchblade drones combine surveillance capabilities like any other drone but can also be used as guided missiles capable of destroying tanks, light armored vehicles, hardened targets, and enemy personnel. The U.S. Army has used Switchblades for over a decade, and their effective deployment by Ukrainian forces has highlighted their broader strategic utility and favorable economics for a less financially resourced military. The United Kingdom is the only other current Switchblade operator, but countries such as like France, Lithuania, and Australia have all signed deals to buy the system since war began in Ukraine.2 Combined, these developments demonstrate how mature and effective drone systems have become and how this market is reshaping warfare.

Artificial Intelligence

AI Excitement Heads to the IPO Market

Cerebras systems, which designs and produces semiconductors, supercomputers, and provides additional AI and software services, filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission to test the public markets. The initial public offering (IPO), which will be the first for a generative AI company, is possible later this year.3 Cerebras differs from most AI chip companies today by taking a Wafer Scale Engine (WSE) approach to AI computing. In a typical chip manufacturing process, a wafer is sliced into multiple smaller chips, acting as an independent processor. Conversely, the WSE approach uses an entire wafer as a single, integrated processing unit. The WSE approach aims to overcome the limitations of conventional chip scaling, with the WSE’s larger size offering potential advantages for AI workloads such as increased parallelism, reduced latency, and memory proximity. The WSE approach gives Cerebras a compelling value proposition for both the training and inference markets. In our view, it has the potential to bring a unique AI hardware play to the market.

Lithium & Battery Technology

U.S. Battery Manufacturing Supply Chain Receives a Boost From Government Investments

The Department of Energy (DOE) announced over $3 billion in funding through the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) to spur the domestic production of advanced batteries and battery materials. In total, the DOE announced funding for 25 projects across 14 states, covering the entire battery manufacturing supply chain, from sourcing and processing critical minerals like lithium, graphite, and manganese to building and recycling batteries.4 Two of the largest grants, up to $225 million each, are for projects extracting lithium from brine. Among these is a joint partnership between Standard Lithium and Equinor that is expected to produce up to 45,000 metric tons of battery-quality lithium carbonate over two decades.5 With batteries essential for renewable energy grids and the electrification of the transportation industry, this funding could help the U.S. expand its role in global battery production.

Healthcare Innovation

Breakthrough Schizophrenia Treatment Gets the Green Light

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved Bristol Myers Squibb’s oral schizophrenia drug for adults, Cobenfy. As the first schizophrenia treatment approval in over 30 years, Cobenfy is a novel pharmacological approach that targets cholinergic receptors as opposed to dopamine receptors, which has long been the norm.6 An estimated 2.8 million people in the United States suffer from schizophrenia, and it is among the top 15 causes of disability worldwide, with patients at higher risk of early death.7,8 While the current standard of care can be effective in managing symptoms, up to 60% of patients experience inadequate improvements in symptoms or intolerable side effects during therapy. In clinical trials, Cobenfy helped manage common schizophrenia symptoms such as hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thinking, potentially providing patients with an effective new alternative.9

Clean Energy

Nuclear Power Gets a Vote of Confidence with Three Miles Island Reopening

Constellation Energy plans to restart the Three Mile Island nuclear plant by 2028, subject to Nuclear Regulatory Commission approval. Constellation, which will invest $1.6 billion to extend the plant’s operations potentially until 2054, signed a 20-year power purchase agreement (PPA) with Microsoft, Constellation’s largest ever PPA.10 Microsoft’s interest reflects a broader trend of tech companies turning to nuclear energy to meet rising electricity needs while adhering to sustainability goals. Microsoft aims to use the plant’s carbon-free electricity to support its growing data center operations, driven by the increasing demands of AI.11 After decades of nuclear plant shutdowns, Three Mile Island’s restart is significant because it signals a revival of nuclear power, a clean and reliable energy source. Increasing demand from sectors like tech and electric vehicles has nuclear energy becoming essential to balance the energy grid and meet future demands. With bipartisan political support, it appears the nuclear energy market has a growing opportunity to achieve those goals.

Data Centers

New AI Clusters With Over 100,000 GPUs Announced

Elon Musk’s xAI launched one of the world’s largest AI training systems, the Colossus supercomputer, which uses 100,000 Nvidia H100 GPUs.12 This system, which xAI brought online in just 122 days, is set to double in size in the coming months with an additional 50,000 Nvidia H200 GPUs. Colossus will power xAI’s efforts to train the upcoming Grok 3 large language model (LLM), requiring up to 150 megawatts (MW) of electricity.13 With global AI chip shortages easing, companies like xAI are able to scale up their systems with larger orders for GPUs. Meta Platforms is also nearing the deployment of its own AI cluster, which features 100,000 Nvidia H100 GPUs to train the next generation of its Llama models. The cost of the GPUs alone could exceed $2 billion.14

The Numbers

The following charts examine returns and sales growth expectations by theme, based on their corresponding ETFs or indices.

To see individual ETF holdings and current performance across the Global X Thematic Suite, including information on the indexes shown, click these links:

data.title: Hershey: Sweeter Than Mondelez This Halloween
data.description:
As Halloween approaches and candy takes over entire aisles in grocery stores, it’s the perfect time to assess two giants of the snack world: Hershey (NYSE:HSY) and Mondelez International (NASDAQ:MDLZ). With iconic brands like Reese’s and Kit Kat, Hershey dominates the U.S. chocolate market, while Mondelez has a larger international presence with names like Oreo and Cadbury. But which company might be the better pick for investors?

In this article, I’ll compare their business models, financial performance, and growth prospects to explain why I see Hershey as the better long-term investment, even if I’d choose a Milka bar over Hershey’s Kisses any day.

Comparing Business Models

HSY is the largest confectioner in the United States, commanding ca. 45% of the chocolate market and with steady expansion into snacks. It operates through three segments:

  • North American Confectionary – Flagship brands include Reese’s, Hershey’s, Kit Kat, Ice Breakers, and Twizzlers.
  • North American Salty Snacks – Key brands include SkinnyPop, Dot’s Pretzels, and Pirate’s Booty.
  • International – Features brands like Pelon Pelo Rico, Lily’s Crispy Rice, and Brookside alongside many of the core U.S. products.

HSY’s business model emphasizes brand loyalty, an extensive distribution network, and strategic acquisitions. It has found significant success in its Salty Snacks segment (e.g., with Dot’s Pretzels) and international growth in markets like India and Brazil, highlighting its ability to diversify beyond chocolate.

By contrast, MDLZ is more heavily focused on global snacks, particularly biscuits and baked items, though it also has a significant portfolio of chocolate products:

  • Biscuits and Baked Snacks – Major brands include Oreo, Chips Ahoy!, Ritz, BelVita, and Triscuit.
  • Chocolate – Key brands include Cadbury, Milka, Toblerone, and Lacta.
  • Gum and Candy – Popular brands include Trident, Dentyne, Halls, and Sour Patch Kids brands.

MDLZ operates on a larger international scale, with a market cap 2.5x that of HSY. This may not be an advantage, however, as it exposes the company to greater risks from currency fluctuations and geopolitical factors.

In short, HSY can leverage its brand loyalty and strong North American base, while MDLZ capitalizes on its global reach and leadership in the snack market.

Comparing Fundamentals

Over the past decade, the two companies have delivered nearly identical total returns. As the chart below illustrates, MDLZ has been the steadier performer, while HSY saw its share price spike during the pandemic before experiencing a ca. 33% in 2023, which Caffital Research attributed to rising cocoa prices, ERP implementation issues, and retailer inventory rebalancing.

Despite these similar past returns, should investors expect equal performance going forward?

HSY has outperformed MDLZ over much of the last decade in terms of revenue and EPS growth, which may signal stronger core momentum. While MDLZ has matched HSY’s growth rate over the past five years, HSY’s long-term consistency stands out:

But while HSY shines in revenue and EPS growth, MDLZ holds an edge in levered free cash flow growth, particularly over the last five years. This can largely be attributed to MDLZ’s focus on cost-cutting and other productivity/efficiency improvements.

In terms of profitability, HSY significantly outperforms MDLZ across several key metrics. Its superior operating and gross margins, and particularly, its higher return on invested capital, suggest it can successfully manage costs and remain profitable even in challenging environments.

Although MDLZ trails HSY in yield, share buybacks give it a modest advantage in total shareholder return. Factoring in buyback yield, which Morningstar calculates at 1.4% for HSY and 2.1% for MDLZ, results in total yields of approximately 4.2% and 4.6% respectively. For some investors, this may be a meaningful difference.

Choosing Hershey over Mondelez

As outlined above, HSY has delivered superior (and more consistent) financial performance, particularly with its higher operating margins and ROIC, and it also offers the more attractive dividend. Building on this, my investment thesis for HSY centers on three key points: brand loyalty, targeted international growth, and long-term stability.

  1. Brand Loyalty – HSY’s dominant position in the U.S. chocolate market combined with its growth in salty snacks shows that it can adapt to changing consumer tastes. Its brands have strong customer loyalty and are ever-present on retail shelves, which can help it weather market pressures. In contrast, as Dividend Power has noted, MDLZ has had to rely more heavily on price increases to drive growth, which may not be as sustainable long-term.
  2. International Growth – While MDLZ has broader global diversification, HSY’s targeted expansion in high-growth markets like India and Brazil is a more measured and possibly less risky approach—one less exposed to geopolitical and currency risks.
  3. Long-Term Stability – Recent challenges such as the new ERP system and rising cocoa prices are likely to be short-term hurdles. HSY’s record of managing similar disruptions suggests the company can remain stable over the long haul. In contrast, MDLZ’s ongoing margin pressures and heightened competition could take longer to resolve.

Regarding Valuations

Currently, MDLZ is trading a slight premium to HSY, with higher trailing and forward P/E ratios. That said, HSY’s earnings are expected to decline by approximately 3% next year, from $9.44 to $9.14 per share, before rebounding to $9.81 in 2026. This equates to a CAGR of around 2% compared to MDLZ’s CAGR of just over 5% through FY26.

Given the downward revisions for HSY, a reasonable share price target for FY25 would be around $182, based on $9.14 earnings at a multiple of 20. This suggests the stock is fairly valued today, at around $184.

If HSY’s valuation returns to its historical five-year average P/E of 24.5, a more optimistic price target would approach $224, representing upside of about 21%. Even this more optimistic target is well below the highs seen in 2023. I also refer readers Miguel Daban’s recent DCF analysis, which arrives at a more conservative fair value of $164.

I’m not bullish on HSY returning to a 24x P/E multiple, which is why I rate HSY a Hold at the current price. But for long-term investors seeking stability and a 4%+ total shareholder yield, HSY remains a solid option at this level.

Hershey: Risks and Challenges

Here are four risks I see that may impact the investment thesis outlined above:

  1. Input Costs – The most immediate concern is volatility in cocoa prices, which directly impacts HSY’s production costs. While the company uses hedging strategies, sustained high prices will inevitably pressure margins.
  2. Labor Costs – Alongside cocoa and sugar prices, labor costs are also climbing. This adds pressure to HSY’s cost structure, and the company may struggle to pass this on to consumers, especially if elevated inflation levels linger.
  3. Slowing Consumer Demand – More of a medium- to long-term risk if inflationary pressures persist. Since inflation usually reduces consumer spending on discretionary items, demand for HSY’s products may weaken as shoppers prioritize essentials.
  4. ERP Implementation Impacts – HSY faced operational challenges in 2023 due to implementation of a new ERP system, which led to delays in manufacturing and delivery. While these issues should be short-term, any ongoing integration problems could continue to weigh on results, as we saw in Q2 2024.

I always recommend reading the company’s most recent 10-K for a detailed discussion of potential risks. See the latest annual report from HSY dated February 20, 2024.

Summary

I see Hershey a solid investment option due to its strong brand loyalty, consistent profitability, and expansion efforts—particularly into the salty snacks market. While Mondelez has broader global exposure, HSY’s higher margins and strong ROIC give it the edge in financial performance. Despite near-term challenges, including rising input costs and operational disruptions, HSY’s targeted international growth and reliable dividend make it attractive for long-term investors seeking stability in their portfolio.

data.title: My Dividend Stock Portfolio: New September Dividend Record - 100 Holdings With 12 Buys
data.description:
Portfolio Changes in September

Investment activity in September reached a multi-year low as I only managed to put around $1,000 into the markets, still predominantly focusing on Business Development Companies. I spent nearly half the month abroad, traveling through Hungary, Albania, and the beautiful Sultanate of Oman for holidays, which I absolutely loved. There's nothing quite like exploring new places, meeting new people, and experiencing different cultures. What made the experience even better, as a dividend investor, is that a significant portion of my travel expenses were covered by dividends, giving me a glimpse of the future. My ultimate goal is to live largely off passive income, and this trip felt like a step toward that vision. It was also humbling to realize that, especially in Oman, living expenses are about 50% lower than in my home country, Germany. Renting a car, paying for gas, and dining out felt like stepping back 10 years or more in terms of affordability. It also highlights that while living off passive income in Germany is still a distant goal, it's already within reach in other countries—especially in Southeast Asia. Thailand, my next destination in 2025, is a perfect example of this, and I can't wait to visit this new country.

Traveling through countries with significantly lower living costs, like Oman, has reshaped my financial perspective. It reinforced the idea that achieving financial independence isn't just about how much you earn, but also where and how you choose to live. Lower expenses can dramatically accelerate progress toward living off passive income. As I look ahead to 2025 with plans to visit Thailand, I'm excited to continue exploring how different regions can support a more financially independent lifestyle. Southeast Asia, with its lower cost of living, offers a unique opportunity to stretch my passive income even further and brings my financial goals closer within reach.

For those on a similar path toward financial independence, my experience has taught me that persistence and a clear vision are key. Every month of dividend growth is a step closer, and by aligning your lifestyle choices with your goals, you can create a future where your passive income not only covers your living expenses but allows you to explore the world on your own terms.

Circling back to the financial markets, I have to admit that I followed developments in September only sporadically and made just a few purchases. It was refreshing to let things run on autopilot, knowing my portfolio is solid enough for me to sleep soundly at night. Coincidentally, the markets surged to new all-time highs, which lit up my portfolio in green across the board but also reduced the number of attractive buying opportunities.

I’ve never aimed to time the market by selling at the top. My approach is simple: I scale back on purchases when valuations feel overly stretched, especially when a small correction seems likely on the horizon.

My main picks were MidCap Financial Investment Corporation (MFIC), Ares Capital (ARCC) and Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD). I'm not expecting any dividend hikes for any of these stocks, but their current dividends are more than sufficient for my investment strategy. Instead, I'm hoping for some special dividends once the Fed starts cutting rates, but I am also more than okay if they choose to retain whatever spillover earnings they have.

If we do get those special dividends, it could bring down stock prices, making the yields more attractive for future purchases. In September, all my moves added $102 to my yearly dividend income, bringing the YTD total up to more than $1,100. My aim is to lift my dividend income by about $100 each month, mostly doable thanks to the high yields from BDCs, and I am currently far ahead of that goal.

Overall, the average yield on cost of my new investments in September averaged around 9.3%, reinforcing my belief in BDCs as a core part of my portfolio. I'm banking on their dividends staying steady - that's what I'm all about when it comes to generating income. Time will tell if I'm on the money or just overly optimistic.

All these purchases break down as follows:

All net purchases in September can be found below:

Dividend Income in September 2024

Dividend income hit a new blistering September record of $1,141. This represents growth of 17% Y/Y and 5% sequentially. These figures are more than impressive to me, but the September 2024 dividend income benefits from the sizable semi-annual dividend payment of the Commonwealth Bank of Australia (OTCPK:CBAUF). Accounting for this factor, Y/Y dividend income rose by 16% and dropped by 1% sequentially.

Disregarding special dividends, on an annual basis, the biggest income driver is by far Ares Capital, a stock I am running at minimum on a bi-weekly savings plan (but most of the time I am buying stock at least once per week) and where my contributions quickly translated into meaningful dividend income. Dividends from Ares have now consistently passed the next big psychologically milestone of $200 with this quarter's dividend income already amounting to $208. Dividend income from MFIC is also rising quickly - although not on the same level as that from ARCC - and has now passed $90.

Similarly striking is that net dividends from monthly dividend payers have now inched ever closer to the $150 level, thanks to the big supplemental dividends from Main Street Capital (MAIN) and Gladstone Investment (GAIN). I am buying more shares of MAIN, GAIN, GLAD and O every month and thus that monthly dividend income level will continue to rise month after month, although the months where the big supplemental dividends hit are truly something special.

In alignment with my investment strategy, I've incorporated a BDC/Non-BDC breakdown into my dividend income charts. This addition allows for more precise monitoring of this critical development.

Additionally, I have created a new visualization that dissects dividend income into two distinct categories: BDCs and Non-BDCs. These categories in turn are separated by dividend payment frequency into Monthly (blue) and Quarterly (orange) in absolute and relative terms.

For Q3 2024, BDCs accounted for approximately one-third of my total dividends, with September alone reflecting a BDC share of 39%. This indicates that September is typically a month heavily focused on BDCs. However, I anticipate a significant drop in this proportion for October, as it will be a light month for BDC dividends.

This metric provides valuable insights and will help guide my investment decisions as it evolves over time.

In September, dividend income from my top three regular payers reached $404, a monstrous jump from last year's average of $271 bringing the YTD average to $381. This tremendous uptick is predominantly driven by MidCap Financial Investment Corporation and Ares Capital.

All this is depicted below:

Here is a chart that shows the development of my net dividend income by month over time between 2015 and 2024. You can see the growth of my dividend income and the average annual dividend for each year:

This chart is my favorite because it illustrates the progression of my dividend income over time and allows me to easily see the average annual dividend for each year.

Next, I have plotted all the individual dividend payments I have received, coloring them by year and arranging the years side by side instead of horizontally as in previous updates. This visualization allows for a more comprehensive analysis of the distribution of my dividend payments over time.

By visualizing the data in this manner, we get a clear picture of how dividend payments are spread out over different years, offering valuable insights into the overall growth of my dividend income over the years.

This visualization is initially quite cluttered, but it contains a wealth of information. It shows every individual dividend payment I have received since I began my investment journey in 2015, represented as a circle that is colored and sized according to its contribution. The view is organized by month and year, allowing for a more comprehensive analysis of the development of my dividend income over time. For each year and month, a white rectangle indicates the average monthly dividend. The area where dividends fall below the average is filled in dark red, while the area above the average is colored dark green.

This type of data visualization allows for a detailed examination of the distribution of my dividend payments over time and enables me to easily identify trends and patterns in my income growth.

Now, zooming in on September, we can immediately see a number of big green circles in a sea of black. The bigger the circle and the bigger the distance to the previous circle for the same stock, the bigger is the change in dividend income compared to the prior years.

The importance of Ares Capital Corporation is clear, with a reddish circle indicating the massive jump in dividend income over the last 12 months due to heavy ongoing monthly investments, and I have no plans to stop these monthly savings plans. By the end of the year, December 2024 dividends could potentially hit $220 or even more.

Apart from that, when it comes to the dividends for 2024, I notice mostly orange circles at the top of the scale, which is exactly what I want to see. These big orange circles represent a rising dividend income, and my goal is to have as many of them as possible at the top of the chart.

Overall, my aim is to observe numerous large orange circles positioned at the highest point on the scale. This signifies a positive trend of increasing dividend income, which is precisely what I'm striving for.

Another way to express the monthly dividend income is in terms of Gifted Working Time (GWT). Assuming an average hourly rate of $36.25 for 2024, my GWT and annual net dividends have been as follows:

  • 2018: 121 hours GWT, $3,000 in annual net dividends
  • 2019: 142 hours GWT, $3,600 in annual net dividends
  • 2020: 152 hours GWT, $3,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2021: 180 hours GWT, $5,050 in annual net dividends
  • 2022: 229 hours GWT, $6,400 in annual net dividends
  • 2023: 272 hours GWT, $8,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2024: Targeting at least 248 hours GWT, $9,000 in annual net dividends

The overall target for the year with $9,000 in annual net dividends is very conservative, but I expect that I will have to take out some risk from my portfolio as I am eyeing to raise capital for real estate investments if I encounter the right deal. Still, as the year unfolds, I do expect to be able to pass that target and potentially even reach my next milestone of $10,000 as well. That would be a great achievement, but nothing I am banking on to reach this year - it would be the icing on the cake.

Across the years, my YTD Dividend Race has been as follows:

  • 2018: Disappointing
  • 2019: Phenomenal, benefiting from a low baseline in the prior year
  • 2020: Fairly disappointing
  • 2021: Phenomenal
  • 2022: Much better than expected, fueled by the very weak euro
  • 2023: Breathtaking
  • 2024: Currently standing at 14% YTD and thus well on track towards achieving the $9,000 milestone. Extrapolating the year-to-date dividends earned from January to September reveals an exciting prospect: surpassing the $9,000 target with ease. With ongoing contributions to my holdings and no further dividend cuts, the outlook is promising. It's entirely reasonable to anticipate an annual dividend income soaring past $9,500 and beyond. Equally exciting is the fact that, with three months remaining in the year, my year-to-date (YTD) dividend income has already surpassed the total dividend income for all of 2022 and is essentially in line with the total dividend income from January to November 2023.

I have updated the YTD Dividend Race chart to show the development of YTD dividend income on a daily basis, allowing us to see that most of my dividend income is generated mid-month and towards the end of the month. The chart also breaks down the development by month for the current year, making it easy to identify significant jumps in income.

Expressed in GWT, it presents itself as follows:

What this shows is as follows:

  1. All time (blue area) - Around 1,423 hours, or 178 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income since the start of my dividend journey. Assuming a five-day workweek, this translates into almost half a year in total.
  2. Full-year 2024 (green bars) - Around 214 hours, or 26.8 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income in 2024, which is basically equivalent to more than five working weeks funded with dividends. That is great progress, and I can't wait to add another month to that statistic.
  3. Highlighted in pink is the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month (September).

This visualization allows us to see the overall impact of my dividend income on the amount of active work I am able to replace with passive income. It also enables us to see the progress made year-to-date, as well as the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month.

Upcoming October Dividends

As every month, October is packed with dividend payments heavily concentrated around the 15th and at month-end when the dividend checks from Canada's big banks as well as JPMorgan will be arriving over a period of just 3-4 days.

Given the majority of my savings plans feature companies that pay dividends in January such as W. P. Carey, Altria (MO), Cisco Systems (CSCO), JPMorgan, Toronto-Dominion Bank, Bank of Nova Scotia, Stryker (SYK), Medtronic (MDT) as well as ongoing investments into stocks like Rithm Capital and Annaly Capital (NLY), it surely is going to be a big month that should easily set a new all-time dividend record for the month of October.

data.title: Latest Memo From Howard Marks: Ruminating On Asset Allocation
data.description:
When I travel to see clients and spend entire days discussing investing and the markets, memo ideas often pop up. Last month's visit with clients in Australia is a case in point. We talked about the "sea change" I believe is taking place in interest rates and about the role of credit in portfolios, and in a few cases, this led to the general topic of asset allocation. The result wasn't a lot of new ideas on the subject, but rather a new way to combine old ideas into a unified theory.

Before I proceed, I want to mention that, from time to time in this memo, I'll say "generally," "usually," or "everything else being equal." These caveats are likely applicable to many more sentences and ideas herein, but for the sake of readability, I'm not going to repeat them ad nauseum. In addition, I'm going to use a lot of graphics, as I truly believe one picture is worth a thousand words. Please bear in mind that these representations are intended to be notional, not technically correct.

Asset Classes

From my vantage point, "asset allocation" is a relatively new thing. No one used that phrase when I joined the industry 55 years ago. Structuring portfolios was a pretty simple matter, generally following the classic "60/40" split. Most U.S. investors limited themselves to investing in U.S. stocks and bonds, and there was a time-honored notion that something like 60% equities and 40% bonds represented reasonable diversification.

Today, investors are presented with so many choices - and there's so much emphasis on getting the decision right - that the term "asset allocation" is very prominent, and there are individuals and whole departments dedicated to doing just that. It's their job to decide how to weight the asset classes to be held in a portfolio, meaning asset allocators spend their time on decisions like these:

  • How much in equities and how much in debt?

  • How much in stocks and bonds and how much in "alternatives"?

  • How much in public securities and how much in private assets?

  • How much in one's home country and how much abroad?

  • How much of the latter in the developed world and how much in emerging markets?

  • How much in high quality assets and how much in low quality?

  • How much in more volatile "high beta" assets and how much in steadier ones?

  • How much in levered strategies and how much unlevered?

  • How much in "real assets"?

  • How much in derivatives?

It's enough to make your head spin. Many investors use computer models to help with these decisions, but the models require inputs regarding expected return, risk, and correlation, and most of these are based on history and thus of questionable relevance to the future. Correlation between asset classes is particularly difficult to predict. It's often a case of garbage in, garbage out (but with the added comfort that comes from using mathematical models).

Ever since coming up with my sea change thesis regarding interest rates two years ago, I've been talking about the increased utility of credit investments. And the more I've done so, the more I've thought about the difference between credit investments and equities. Thus, the first thing I want to mention about my "Australian epiphany" is the unconventional idea that, at bottom, there are only two asset classes: ownership and debt. If someone wants to participate financially in a business, the essential choice is between (a) owning part of it and (b) making a loan to it.

When I moved from Citibank's equity research department to its bond department in 1978, I learned firsthand that this is a matter of night and day. On my new desk, I found a machine called a Monroe 360/65 Bond Trader. If you typed in a bond's interest rate, maturity date, and market price, it would tell you the yield to maturity . . . in other words, what your return would be if you bought the bond at that price and held it to maturity (and it paid). This was revolutionary to me. On the equity side I'd come from, there was no place you could look to find out what your return would be.

This highlighted for me something I've always felt most investors don't grasp viscerally: the essential difference between stocks and bonds . . . that is, between ownership and lending. Investors seem to think of stocks and bonds as two things that fall under the same heading. But the difference is enormous. In fact, ownership and lending have nothing in common:

  • Owners put their money at risk with no promise of a return. They acquire a piece of a business or other asset and are entitled to their proportional share of any residual that remains after the necessary payments have been made to employees, providers of raw materials, landlords, tax authorities, and, of course, lenders. If there's something left over, it's called profit or cash flow, and the owners have the right to share in whatever part of it is paid out. And if there's profit or cash flow (or the potential for it in the future), the business will have "enterprise value," in which the owners also share.

  • Lenders typically provide funds to help owners purchase or operate businesses or other assets and, in exchange, are promised periodic interest and the repayment of principal at the end. The relationship between borrower and lender is contractual, and the resulting return is known in advance as described above, again assuming the borrower makes the promised payments when due. That's why this kind of investing is called "fixed income" - the income is fixed. For the purposes of this memo, however, it might help to think of it as "fixed outcome" investing.

This isn't a difference in degree; it's a difference in kind. Ownership assets (things like common stocks, whole companies, real estate, private equity, and real assets) and debt (bonds, loans, mortgage backed securities, and other streams of promised payments) should be thought of as entirely different, not variations on a theme. They have different characteristics and potential, and the choice between them is one of the most basic things investors must decide.

The Essential Choice

At the outset of this memo, I listed some of the decisions that comprise the asset allocation process. But how can those decisions be approached? What's the framework for making them?

The next piece that clicked into place in my thinking "down under" was with regard to the basic characteristics of a portfolio. In my opinion, one decision matters more than - and should set the basis for - all the other decisions in the portfolio management process. It's the selection of a targeted "risk posture," or the desired balance between aggressiveness and defensiveness. The essential decision in investing is how much emphasis one should put on preserving capital and how much on growing it. These two things are mostly mutually exclusive:

  • Insistence on preserving capital - or, secondarily, on limiting the portfolio's volatility - calls for an emphasis on defense, which precludes pursuing maximum growth.

  • Correspondingly, a decision to strive to maximize growth requires an emphasis on offense, meaning preservation of capital and steadiness must be sacrificed to some degree.

It's one or the other. You can't simultaneously emphasize both preservation of capital and maximization of growth, or defense and offense. This is the fundamental, inescapable truth in investing. The questions listed on page one are just details, the options available for reaching your targeted risk posture.

If you think about portfolio construction in this sense - looking for the right balance between offense and defense - it becomes clear that the goal should be optimization, not maximization. To my mind, it shouldn't be "wealth," but "wealth pursued in an appropriate way, taking into account the investor's wants and needs."

Many people think the proper goal in investing is achieving the highest return. More sophisticated thinkers understand - either intellectually or intuitively - that the goal should be to achieve the best relationship between return and risk. If you follow that latter mandate, it'll hopefully lead you to assets whose expected return is more than sufficient to compensate for their risk, and thus to a portfolio with the potential for an attractive risk-adjusted return. But that's not enough.

The absolute level of risk in a portfolio shouldn't be an unwitting consequence of the asset allocation process described above, or of the search for superior risk-adjusted returns. The absolute risk level must be consciously targeted. In fact, in my view, it's the most important thing. For an investment program to be successful, the level of risk in the portfolio must be well compensated and fall within the desired range . . . neither too much nor too little.

The Shape of the Curves

In the last few months, I've been drawing probability distributions to illustrate the fundamental difference between the potential returns from ownership assets and debt (or "fixed income," "credit," or whatever you want to call it). Here's the general shape of the curve describing the potential return on a portfolio of ownership assets (Figure 1):

And following on page four is the shape of the curve describing the potential return on a portfolio of debt (Figure 2):

Ownership assets typically have a higher expected return, greater upside potential, and greater downside risk. Everything else being equal, the expected returns from debt are lower but likely to fall within a much tighter range. There's generally no upside on debt - no one should buy an 8% bond expecting to make more than 8% per year over the long term. But there's also relatively little downside - you'll get your 8% if the borrower pays, and relatively few fail to pay. For this reason, offense is usually better played through ownership assets, and defense is usually better played through debt. (I hasten to add that investing isn't a matter of either/or. The two can be combined, meaning the operative question surrounds the right mix.)

In the low-interest-rate environment that prevailed from 2009 through 2021, the expected return from debt was extremely low in the absolute and far below the historical return on equities, rendering debt relatively unattractive (Figure 3).

But today, it's considerably higher than it was and closer to that of equities (Figure 4). That's why I've been urging increased investment in credit.

Obviously, the relationship between the two curves at a point in time has a very direct bearing on the appropriate asset allocation at that time.

Which of the two is "better," ownership or debt? We can't say. In a market with any degree of efficiency - that is, rationality - it's just a tradeoff. A higher expected return with further upside potential, at the cost of greater uncertainty, volatility, and downside risk? Or a more dependable but lower expected return, entailing less upside and less downside? The choice between the two is subjective, largely a function of the investor's circumstances and attitude toward bearing risk. That means the answer will be different for different investors.

Choosing the Offense/Defense Balance

I've previously expressed my view that, as a starting point, every investor or their investment manager should identify their appropriate normal risk posture or offense/defense balance. For each individual or institution, this decision should be informed by the investor's investment horizon, financial condition, income, needs, aspirations, responsibilities, and, crucially, intestinal fortitude, or their ability to stomach ups and downs.

Once investors have specified the normal risk posture that's right for them, they face a choice: they can maintain that posture all the time, or they can opt to depart from it on occasion in response to the movements of the market and thus changes in the attractiveness of the offerings it provides, increasing their emphasis on offense when the market is beaten down and on defense when it's riding high.

Regardless of whether one's risk posture is fixed or variable, however, the next question is how one gets there. This question led me to think about another old idea: the relationship between risk and return. I've described a million times the way this was taught at the University of Chicago, beginning when I was there in the 1960s. It's a graphical presentation we've all seen ever since, in which, as we move from left to right, increasing the expected risk, the expected return also increases (Figure 5):

As readers know, I always felt this representation was highly inadequate, since the linearity of the relationship in the graph makes it appear too certain that increased risk will lead to increased return. This obviously belies the nature of risk. So, in a memo in 2006, I took the same line and superimposed on it some bell-shaped curves representing probability distributions turned on their side. I did this to indicate the uncertain nature of returns from riskier assets (Figure 6):

Now, we see that as the thing called "risk" increases (that is, as we move from left to right on the graph), not only does the expected return increase, but the range of possible outcomes becomes wider and the bad outcomes become worse. That's risk! (I hope this way of presenting risk will be considered a lasting contribution to the investment industry when I'm gone.)

Doodling one day, I took the black and green curves describing ownership asset returns and debt returns from Figure 4 and added some intermediate positions in blue and red to indicate various combinations of the two. Thus, the blue curve is 2/3 debt and 1/3 ownership, and the red is 1/3 debt and 2/3 ownership (Figure 7):

In Australia, as I was showing this diagram, it struck me that Figure 7 is just another way to represent the idea presented in Figure 6. Again, as we move from left to right (more ownership assets, less debt), the expected return increases and the expected risk increases (that is, just as in Figure 6, the range of possible outcomes grows wider and the left-hand tail stretches further into undesirable territory). This way of presenting the options might be more intuitively clear.

Someone who believes in "more risk, more return" as portrayed in Figure 5 should logically adopt a high-risk posture. But if they understand the real implications of increased risk, as suggested by Figures 6 and 7, then they might opt for something more moderate.

The Role of Alpha and Beta

All the foregoing assumes markets are efficient:

  • As risk increases in an efficient market, expected return increases proportionally. Or maybe that's better stated the other way around: as expected return increases, so does the accompanying risk (the uncertainty surrounding the outcome and the likelihood of a bad one). Thus, no position on the risk continuum (for example, in Figure 6) is "better" than any other. It's all just a matter of where you want to come out in terms of absolute riskiness, or what absolute level of return you want to aim for. The ratio of return to risk is similar at all points on the continuum - less of both toward the left, and more of both toward the right. Said another way, there's no free lunch.

  • Also, looking at each position on the risk continuum, the symmetricalness of the vertical distribution of possible returns around the expected return is similar from one position to the next. That means the ratio of upside potential to downside risk at one position on the continuum isn't markedly better than it is at other positions - again no free lunch.

  • Finally, if you want to move further out on the risk continuum, you can do so by either (a) investing in riskier assets or (b) applying leverage to the same assets (magnifying both the expected return and risk). Again, in a fully efficient market, neither tactic is preferable to the other.

The above three statements capture some of the important implications of supposed market efficiency.

Looked at this way, the only thing that matters is getting to the right risk position for you; under an assumption of market efficiency, there's nothing to be gained in terms of return at a given level of risk. All ways of getting to a certain risk level will produce the same expected return.

The reason for this is the academic view that, in an efficient market, (a) all assets are priced fairly relative to each other, such that there are no bargains or over-pricings to take advantage of and (b) there's no such thing as alpha, which I define as "gains resulting from superior individual skill." As a result, there's nothing to be gained from active decision making: no asset class, strategy, security or manager is "better" than any other. They merely vary in terms of risk and resulting return.

Also in the academic view, since there's no such thing as alpha, the only thing that differentiates assets is their beta, or their relative volatility, the extent to which they reflect market movements. In the theory, it's beta that expected returns are proportional to.

Now it's time for me to assert strenuously that, in reality, markets are not efficient in the academic sense of always being "right." Markets may do an efficient job of (a) rapidly incorporating new information and (b) accurately reflecting the resulting consensus opinion concerning the right price for each asset given the totality of information, but that opinion can be far from correct. For that reason, gains can be achieved by choosing skillfully among the options:

  • some assets, markets or strategies can offer a better risk/return bargain than others, and

  • some managers can operate within a market or strategy to produce superior risk-adjusted returns.

This last idea raises one of the key questions in asset allocation: should you consider departing from your "sweet spot" in terms of risk level in order to invest in a riskier asset class with a manager believed to possess alpha? There's no easy answer to this question, especially given that many managers who are believed to possess alpha turn out not to.

To conclude, I'll recap the key points:

  • Fundamentally speaking, the only asset classes are ownership and debt.

  • They differ enormously in terms of their fundamental nature.

  • Ownership assets and debt assets should be combined to get your portfolio to the position on the risk/return continuum that's right for you. This is the most important decision in portfolio management or asset allocation.

  • The other decisions are merely a matter of implementation.

  • Of course, your asset allocation process will be informed by how you rate your ability to identify and access superior strategies and superior managers, recognizing that doing so isn't easy.

Moving on to the real world, I want to make some important observations regarding one of Oaktree's key sectors, non-investment grade credit (defined as performing non-government debt):

  • The prospective returns in this area today are much higher than they were in the 2009-21 period.

  • These returns, starting at roughly 7% on public credit and 10% on private credit, are competitive with the historical returns on equities and capable of helping many investors toward their overall return targets.

  • Because of their contractual nature, the returns from credit are likely to prove much more dependable than ownership returns.

In my view, the thought process set forth in this memo leads to the conclusion that investors should increase their allocations in this area if they are (a) attracted by returns of 7-10% or so, (b) desirous of limiting uncertainty and volatility, and (c) willing to forgo upside potential beyond today's yields to do so. For me, that should include a lot of investors, even if not everyone.

My recommendation at this time is that investors do the research required to increase their allocation to credit, establish a "program" for doing so, and take a partial step to implement it. While today's potential returns are attractive in the absolute, higher returns were available on credit a year or two ago, and we could see them again if markets come to be less ruled by optimism. I believe there will be such a time.

Thank you for indulging me in this foray into investment philosophy. I hope you've found it of value.

data.title: A Fat 15% Yield With Monthly Income For My Retirement Portfolio: XFLT
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

Every once in a while, there is a must-own or must-buy item for Christmas, where one product becomes extremely popular and a major draw for consumers. During the 1990s, three of the most popular must-own toys were Furby, Tickle Me Elmo, and Ninja Turtle-related toys due to the popularity of their films and TV shows.

Interestingly, you can still buy Furbies today, but they've been relegated to the toy shelf along with all the other toys that have come and gone. Yet, during the peak of their popularity, people were pushing, fighting, shoving, and losing their Christmas spirit in the drive to get this must-own gift. Every single Christmas, there's a new recommended must-owned toy for kids that year.

When it comes to the market, investors frequently feel that there are these new must-buy picks that float around. While I think many great opportunities come and go, I view them more as a trading opportunity than an investment. If it's something that I must buy today, and it's going to be something different next week, then likely it's not an investment, but a swing trade.

There's nothing wrong with swing trading if that's what you enjoy, but it is just a much riskier proposition than I would recommend for the average retiree or novice approaching the market. Instead, I like to have long-term investments, something that I can buy now and hold for decades to come, all while collecting a wonderful income. I view these more as the tent pegs of my retirement plan. They're there to secure my retirement and hold it fast, regardless of what economic winds come my way. They can beat my retirement plan, but with that tent peg securely fastened to the ground, it'll be holding my retirement plan tent in place. I don't depend on a single tent peg. I depend on 42 different tent pegs at minimum, helping make my retirement plan as secure as possible.

Today, I want to examine one of those tent pegs that, I think, is worth driving into the ground to secure your retirement plan.

Let's dive in!

High yields, Lower Risks

XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (NYSE:XFLT), yielding 14.9%, is a CEF that invests in leveraged loans. These are loans originated by banks and then are usually sold to investors. Selling a loan after origination allows banks to ensure they have cash on hand to lend at the next opportunity.

There are different ways to invest in these loans. XFLT can buy them directly, meaning that XFLT is directly exposed to the economic benefits of owning the loan. As the borrower pays interest or repays/prepays principal, XFLT is paid directly proportionally to the amount of the loan they own. If you look at an ETF like Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN), that fund holds the same types of loans.

Another way to gain exposure to leveraged loans is through a CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligation). A CLO is a mini-fund that is actively managed. The CLO manager raises capital, buys up leveraged loans, and then launches a CLO. CLOs are funded by various levels of debt and an equity tranche. They utilize the “waterfall” method to distribute cash flow, where the most senior tranches get paid an agreed amount before the next tranche gets paid. The equity tranche gets any excess, in exchange for taking on the risk of absorbing any defaults. This structure allows very risk-adverse investors like banks and insurance companies to pay a premium and get the most senior tranches. Meanwhile, more aggressive investors can receive much larger returns when default rates are low with the equity tranches.

XFLT takes a balanced approach to the leveraged loan market, with approximately half of its portfolio invested into loans and bonds, and the other half invested indirectly in loans through CLOs. Source.

It is worth noting that XFLT has been favoring CLO equity over CLO debt tranches. The reason is that CLO debt prices have gone up. CLO assets and CLO debt are both floating-rate, but with the aggressive hiking cycle, even floating-rate debt was not immune. Floating rate debt is stated as SOFR plus a spread. For example, in Q4 2023, the safest AAA tranches of CLOs were averaging SOFR + 175 bps. So if SOFT is 5.5%, the AAA tranche would be paid 5.5% + 1.75% = 7.75%. In Q2 of 2024, AAA tranches were being priced at an average of SOFR + 150 bps. In other words, a CLO issued in Q2 2024 was paying 0.25% lower interest expense than a deal issued in Q4 2023.

Going back further, in 2022/2023, there were deals with the AAA tranches priced north of SOFR + 200 bps. So, a CLO being issued today is paying meaningfully lower interest payments to the senior tranches, which means more available for the equity tranche.

The way a CLO is structured, the equity tranche is entitled to call the debt tranches and refinance or reset the CLO. Refinancing means repaying a debt tranche and taking out the same amount of new debt. Resetting means refinancing all the debt a CLO has and setting new terms like new call dates, resetting the reinvestment period, and changing the date when the CLO starts to liquidate and repay lenders.

With spreads compressing in the debt tranches, equity holders have been very active in refinancing and resetting debt tranches.

XFLT management has recognized this opportunity, and as the debt tranches it bought when spreads were wider are repaid, it is focusing more on CLO equity opportunities. This is a major benefit of a fund that is diversified and willing to take multiple approaches. Instead of committing to invest only in debt tranches or only in equity, XFLT adapts to changing conditions and invests in what management sees as the best opportunities in current conditions.

For investors like us, XFLT's diversification means that we are accepting a lower yield than we might get from a fund that focuses solely on CLO equity. CLO equity is the highest-yielding, but also the highest-risk, strategy in this sector. Yet XFLT's current yield is nothing to sneeze at, and we saw during COVID-19 that even in the face of a Black Swan, the negative impacts on the distribution lasted for only two quarters. This makes XFLT an excellent option for investors who want a taste of the sky-high yields available in CLO equity today but want to temper it with some stability.

Conclusion

XFLT drives a unique path between being extremely exposed to CLO equity, like OXLC or ECC, and being overly focused on individual loans, like BKLN. By driving this middle path and being willing to be flexible in its holdings to adapt to the best potential opportunities at the time, we're able to benefit from the active management that oversees its portfolio. The interesting part is that the portfolio of XFLT is primarily run by one of the best-rated CLO managers. This gives you unique exposure to a team that oversees CLOs across the market.

When it comes to retirement, having a steady supply of monthly income can make budgeting so much easier. Well, I love quarterly dividends, and I will not reject buying something if it doesn't pay a monthly dividend. I enjoy receiving my monthly income and using that to match my bills directly. For many novice income investors, having a dividend stream that covers your phone bill, power bill, and mortgage can be a powerful way to measure the real-world benefit of growing that income stream month to month and year to year. Having a fixed income in retirement and a steadily growing stream of dividend income can provide you with countless opportunities that you otherwise would not have access to. The more dividend income you get, the more relaxing many aspects of your financial health will be because there'll be less stress.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Flotek Industries: Scratched And Dented, Still A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Continuing our "Scratch and Dent" series, we return to Flotek Industries, (NYSE:FTK). The company has been fairly busy since our last article with a 1-6 reverse stock split to meet NYSE listing requirements in September 2023, an improving bill book and balance sheet, increased sales of the CnF well stimulation fluids, and some inroads on their optical gas measurement device, JP-3. Unlike many OFS companies, more things seem to be going right than wrong.

The stock rallied nearly 100% in the second quarter to $5.40 per share, but had slowly been giving some of that back in the third quarter, before another rally began in late September. I expect the decline is related to the crash in oil prices, but increasingly the company's revenue stream isn't tied solely to the rig count. Realization of that fact may be what accounts for the October rally back toward $5.00, just below which it now sits. Accordingly, we must take a look at what drove Q-2's results, and see if that may extend into Q-3, which FTK will report on November 4th.

We can't really claim a win after recommending it at a pre-split $0.77, as post-split and discounting the rallies, the stock is right where it was. Still, this is a unique player in our coverage with novel chemistry that has increasing application as gas and water cuts become more problematic, and an equipment line - JP-3 that delivers subscription-type revenue.

Could the macro market be changing in FTK's favor? Let's have a look.

Catalysts for FTK in a horrific upstream market

You don't need me to tell you that things are all "pear-shaped," in the upstream OFS market. We've discussed the bifurcation-operators are doing ok with $70 oil, but companies reliant on oilfield activity are struggling as drilling and fracking stagnate. Witness what Schlumberger, (SLB) told us the other day.

Weak oil and gas prices have caused "a cautionary approach to activity and discretionary spend by many customers," CEO Olivier Le Peuch said on SLB's earnings conference call.

And, SLB actually guided higher on International revenue-80% of its total, and margins for the year. It's made no difference as perception equals reality, SLB stock is down 50% YoY. If the King of all OFS companies is struggling, then it would seemingly be understandable that a tiny player like FTK is as well, except that it makes no sense. Like SLB, Flotek is seeing higher revenues and margins from technology sales.

You will recall our discussion on Select Water Solutions, (WTTR) and the opportunities it has in managing produced water-at the surface. Given the magnitude of the problem, you can probably imagine the interest that an oil operator would express in a proposal to minimize the amount of water cut in production. Keeping water in the ground means not having to deal with it at the surface, and that brings value for which people will pay.

At a high level, what FTK is doing in this analysis is applying destabilizing agents - CnFs and surfactants to prevent emulsions from forming as oil and aqueous fluids mix. Emulsions are oil-wet externally, so this is also a wettability problem. Prescriptive Chemistry Management means keeping the pores water-wet encourages more oil production with a lower water cut.

More oil, less water is the takeaway from the slide above. CEO Ryan Ezell discusses the YoY improvement in prescriptive chemistry sales in the Q-2 call:

Notably, we saw an 89% increase in our proprietary Complex nano-Fluids technology sales in the first half of 2024 versus the first half of 2023.

Flotek will remain at the forefront of innovation and multidisciplinary advancements as we bring new technologies to the market, including AI-driven reservoir modeling to address the impacts of water inhibition, drive preferential microfluid behavior in nanopore environments and improve the ultimate recovery of hydrocarbons from each asset.

Logic suggests that we will hear more of this come the November 5th CC.

Moving on to what the company describes as Data Analytics

Flare monitoring is the low-hanging fruit in what FTK calculates as a $220 mm TAM, annual market. Optical measurement is an improvement over traditional GC/MS type equipment that requires physical sampling to enable analysis.

There are a number of obvious markets for expansion of the JP-3 analyzer business. Two they highlight in this presentation are Custody Transfer and Field Power Generation.

The Custody Transfer application is present in every well to gathering line sale. How much, what is the BTU content, and are there liquids in the gas which need to be accounted for, are all relevant questions as oil and gas move toward storage or refining.

Most frack pumps are now Dual Fuel-meaning they run on a mix of diesel and field gas. Maximizing the field gas saves more expensive diesel, and more importantly, reduces the carbon footprint of fracking.

Ryan Ezell, CEO, comments on the growth of Data Analytics in Q-2:

Our Data Analytics segment revenue increased 22% in the first quarter of 2024. We remain focused on converting to a Data as a Service model, combined with the launch of our next-generation measurement system, unlocking significant upstream market opportunities as we expect the business to see continued growth during the third quarter.

My take on these two services is that both have a long runway for continued growth. Opportunities to help operators improve production profiles are generally easy sells-I know this from my SLB experience. The same drivers exist for meeting government imperatives with regard to regulatory adherence, and in other commercial applications they have outlined for JP-3 Verax units.

Q-2, 2024 and guidance

  • Generated total revenue of $46.2 million, a 14% increase from the first quarter of 2024, driven by a 40% sequential increase in chemistry revenues from external customers and a 22% sequential increase in Data Analytics revenue.
  • Increased gross profit margin and adjusted gross profit margin(1) to 20% and 23%, respectively, as compared to 8% and 10% respectively, during the second quarter 2023.
  • Reported net income of $2.0 million compared to a net loss of $21 thousand for the second quarter of 2023 and delivered a year-over-year increase in adjusted EBITDA(1) of $6.4 million.
  • Amended Asset Based Loan agreement increases loan commitment by 45% to $20 million and reduces the interest rate.
  • Received approval from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) of the JP3 analyzer system for use in flare emission monitoring, facilitating access to a new upstream market application with an estimated annual total addressable market of $220 million.

2024 Guidance: Stronger Profitability Expectations

As a result of the Company's strong operational performance during the first half of 2024 and the outlook for the balance of the year, the Company is raising its 2024 profitability guidance. Flotek now expects adjusted EBITDA(2) to be in a range of between $14 million and $18 million, a 23% increase to the midpoint of the range when compared to the prior guidance range of between $10 million and $16 million. (Source)

Risks

The key risk in the short term for FTK is the continued weakness in oilfield activity as operators "right-size" their drilling and stimulation activity in the post-M&A craze that has resulted in a smaller market. The company has a take or pay deal (+/- $8.0 mm per quarter) with its key customer-ProFrac, (ACDC) for frac chemicals-friction reducers, clay stabilizers, and emulsion preventers, but would be better served by servicing a larger number of units. I see the primary outcome in this scenario as FTK being dead money from poor sentiment in the upstream space. This is pretty well priced in my view, so I don't see a lot of downside risk from here.

Your takeaway

It takes a bit of a leap of faith to consider an investment in FTK in the current climate. In this article, I've tried to show that they have substantial revenue generation potential that's not directly linked to the rig count.

Here's what we know. The four analysts covering the company rate it a buy, with price targets as high as $7.00. EPS estimates are for $0.07 in Q-3, and $0.09 in Q-4. They blew away the $0.05 estimate for Q-2, turning in an impressive $0.12. If they were to repeat that performance in Q-3, EBITDA would rise toward $14 mm on an annualized basis. That's about 60% above their current run rate, and would justify the $7.00 price target for the company. If we were to extend that performance to Q-4 numbers, we could see further uplift in the share price.

Accordingly, we renew our buy rating on FTK and think investors looking for speculative upside may wish to consider taking a position in the company at current levels. I think the macro themes I've outlined here are real and not tied to the rig count, and give the company an ample opportunity to increase revenues and profits.
